metadata.title: Four Layers, Five Hard Stops: The Framework
metadata.playlist: Build the Recipe — Make Your Tool Conductor-Ready
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5B brief — Nik's Recipe-Based Framework

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 3 of 6. This episode has no deliverable. It is orientation. Before you write the recipe in Episode 4, you need to understand the architecture you are writing inside. The recipe is not a description of steps — it is a contract between the pipeline and the conductor, where the conductor is whoever runs it: Claude Code, a colleague, or yourself six months from now. The contract has four layers. At every layer boundary, there is a handoff with a typed judgment call. There are five types of judgment call — and every phase gate in the recipe must name which type it is. Understanding those five types before you write a single recipe step is what prevents you from writing a gate that says 'human checks here' with no information about what kind of check to make. Let's go.

props.segment: Four Layers, Five Hard Stops: The Framework
props.command: Understand the 4-layer architecture and 5 judgment types before writing the recipe.
props.runningText: no deliverable — orientation only; this is what E4 depends on…
output: 5 judgment types: PA · PF · TO · IJ · EI
Every phase gate names one type — no typeless gates

beats.act: four layers and the conductor's job at each
beats.narration_text: The pipeline has four layers, and the conductor has a different question at each one. Layer one: external sources. These are APIs, RSS feeds, datasets — anything outside the pipeline's control. The conductor's question here is: did the source give us what we expected? Rate limits, format changes, missing fields — all of these happen at the external layer. Never trust external data without checking it. Layer two: data/raw/. This is the ingest landing zone. Raw data sits here before any gate has been applied. The conductor's question is the IJ — Ingest Judgment — question: is this raw pull within the expected range? Is the record count reasonable? Are the formats as expected? Layer three: data/verified/. This is where GIGO-passed records live. The conductor's question is: did the records meet the quality gate from the config? Anything that passes arrives here; anything that fails is documented in the README. Layer four: logs/reports/. This is where the pipeline's output goes — the things a human user will actually see and act on. The conductor's question is the TO — Tool Output Review — question: does this output make sense for the primary user?

props.artifactHeading: The conductor's question at each layer
artifactLines: External sources: 'Did the source give us what we expected?' — format, range, rate limits.
data/raw/: IJ — 'Is this raw pull within expected range?' — count, format, completeness.
data/verified/: 'Did records meet the quality gate?' — GIGO passed only; failures documented.
logs/reports/: TO — 'Does this output make sense for the primary user?' — review before use.
Rule: every handoff is a contract. Every contract has a question. Every question has a type.
props.sparkLine: External → raw/ → verified/ → reports/. Different question at each.

beats.act: five judgment types — hard stops, not optional check-ins
beats.narration_text: The five judgment types are PA, PF, TO, IJ, and EI. They are hard stops — not suggestions, not optional check-ins. When a gate with one of these types fires, a human must make a decision before the pipeline proceeds. PA — Pipeline Approval. Fires when the conductor must decide whether the run is safe to start or continue. An example: before the first data pull, the conductor confirms the sources are accessible and the quality gate criteria still match the brief. PF — Pipeline Failure. Fires when something broke badly enough that the run cannot continue. An example: the AI API returned a 500 error on every request in the batch — the run stops, the conductor investigates. TO — Tool Output Review. Fires when the AI produced output that must be checked by a human before it goes to the user. An example: the AI generated a report that will be sent to a primary user — the conductor reads it before it sends. IJ — Ingest Judgment. Fires at the raw-to-verified boundary. An example: the RSS feed returned eighty records when it normally returns twenty — the conductor decides whether the unusually large pull is valid or whether something went wrong at the source. EI — Exception Intervention. Fires on anything unexpected that does not fit the other four. An example: a record appears to contain personally identifying information that should not be in this dataset. The pipeline stops and a human decides what to do.

artifactLines: PA — Pipeline Approval: is it safe to start or continue? (pre-run or mid-run gate).
PF — Pipeline Failure: something broke — run cannot continue; human investigates.
TO — Tool Output Review: AI output reviewed by human before it reaches the user.
IJ — Ingest Judgment: raw pull within expected range? Count/format/completeness check.
EI — Exception Intervention: unexpected situation; human decides what to do.
These are HARD STOPS — not suggestions. Human decides before pipeline proceeds.
props.sparkLine: Hard stops. Human decides. Pipeline waits.

beats.act: accountable ai — why this is a hireable skill
beats.narration_text: Most people who build AI pipelines never define failure modes before they run. They wire a workflow, click Execute, and see what happens. If it breaks, they fix it. If the AI produces bad output, they notice eventually. The Path B framework forces a different discipline: define what good looks like, define what bad looks like, name the type of judgment required at every handoff, and build the hard stop before the first run. That is accountable AI. It is the same discipline as this video pipeline's own phase gates — GATE P says define good before spending, GATE M says a human approves before publishing. The five judgment types encode the same idea in a machine-readable recipe. A hiring manager who sees a system with explicit hard stops and typed judgment gates is looking at someone who thought about failure before it happened. That is not the default. Most demos do not show error paths. Yours will. That is what makes it a portfolio artifact.

artifactLines: Most AI pipelines: wire it → run it → fix it when it breaks.
Path B: define good-looks-like · define bad-looks-like · name the judgment type · build the stop first.
Same discipline as GATE P (define good before spend) and GATE M (human approves before publish).
A hiring manager sees typed hard stops → 'this person thought about failure before it happened.'
Most demos show only the happy path. Yours shows the failure paths. That's the portfolio artifact.
props.sparkLine: Define failure before you run. That's the rare thing.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Four Layers, Five Hard Stops. No deliverable today — this is the architecture you write the recipe inside. Four layers: external sources, data/raw/, data/verified/, and logs/reports/. The conductor has a different question at each layer boundary. Five judgment types: PA for start or continue decisions, PF for pipeline failures that stop the run, TO for AI output that needs human review before use, IJ for ingest range and format checks, and EI for anything unexpected. These are hard stops — not optional check-ins. Every phase gate in the recipe must name one of the five types. A gate without a type is incomplete and costs ten points. Episode 4 is the recipe.

props.artifactTitle: Four Layers, Five Hard Stops: The Framework
artifactLines: 4 layers: External → data/raw/ → data/verified/ → logs/reports/.
Conductor's question at each: did source deliver? / ingest within range? / passed gate? / output makes sense?
5 types: PA (start/continue) · PF (failure/stop) · TO (output review) · IJ (ingest check) · EI (exception).
Hard stops — human decides before pipeline proceeds.
No type = incomplete = -10 pts per gate. Every gate names one type.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. No output to build yet — but here is the thinking exercise that makes Episode 4 faster. Paste this into Claude: My Madison tool has these layer transitions: ingest from external sources to data/raw/, GIGO check from data/raw/ to data/verified/, and AI processing from data/verified/ to logs/reports/. For each transition, name which of the five judgment types — PA, PF, TO, IJ, EI — applies and write one sentence explaining why that type and not the others. Use these definitions: PA is a start-or-continue decision, PF is a failure requiring stop, TO is AI output requiring human review before use, IJ is an ingest range or format check, EI is an unexpected situation requiring human decision. Also identify any transition where two judgment types might apply — for example a pipeline that fails catastrophically at ingest could be both IJ and PF. For any dual-type case, tell me which type is primary and why. Work through this mapping before you open the recipe template. The recipe phase gates are easier to write when you already know which type applies to each handoff.

props.segment: Four Layers, Five Hard Stops: The Framework
props.command: My tool's layer transitions: [list them]. For each: which of PA/PF/TO/IJ/EI applies and why? Flag any transition where two types might apply — name the primary type and explain the choice.
props.runningText: map the judgment types before opening the recipe template — gates are faster to write when the type is already decided…

beats.narration_text: Four Layers, Five Hard Stops: The Framework. Liam, in for Bear.
